description:
You are a data-scientist who inherited a mess of symbolic links that point to several CSV
        datasets.  Your goal is to reorganize the structure so that only valid, clearly-named
        links remain and that an inventory log is produced.

        Directory layout that already exists:
        1. /home/user/projects/datasets/raw/  
           ├── sales_2020.csv  
           ├── sales_2021.csv  
           └── customers_2020.csv  

        2. /home/user/projects/datasets/links/  
           ├── old_sales        -> ../raw/sales_2019.csv   (broken; target file does NOT exist)  
           └── cust_link        -> ../raw/customers_2020.csv (valid)  

        What you must accomplish (≈ 5-10 shell commands in total):

        A. Create a new directory /home/user/projects/datasets/clean_links/.  
           • It must end up containing **only three** symbolic links, whose **names and targets**
             are exactly as follows (all links must be *relative*):  
             1. clean_sales_2020  -> ../raw/sales_2020.csv  
             2. clean_sales_2021  -> ../raw/sales_2021.csv  
             3. clean_customers_2020 -> ../raw/customers_2020.csv  

        B. Remove:
           • The entire /home/user/projects/datasets/links directory (including the broken links).

        C. Produce an audit log file /home/user/projects/link_audit.log with this precise format:
           Every line must contain:  
           <link_path><TAB><arrow><TAB><target_path><NEWLINE>  
           where  
           • <link_path>  = absolute path to the symlink,  
           • <arrow>      = the literal string  "->",  
           • <target_path>= absolute path that the link resolves to (not the relative string in the
                            link but the real, canonical target file),  
           • Columns are separated by a single TAB character (Unicode 0x09).  
           • Lines must appear **alphabetically sorted by <link_path>**.  
           • No extra blank lines.  
           After finishing this task, the log must therefore contain exactly three lines, e.g.:

           /home/user/projects/datasets/clean_links/clean_customers_2020<TAB>-><TAB>/home/user/projects/datasets/raw/customers_2020.csv
           /home/user/projects/datasets/clean_links/clean_sales_2020<TAB>-><TAB>/home/user/projects/datasets/raw/sales_2020.csv
           /home/user/projects/datasets/clean_links/clean_sales_2021<TAB>-><TAB>/home/user/projects/datasets/raw/sales_2021.csv

        D. Verify that no broken symlinks exist anywhere under /home/user/projects after you
           finish.

        Only the log file and the three new symlinks in clean_links/ should remain; everything
        else must be untouched or removed exactly as described.

        NOTE:
        • Do not move or edit the CSV files in raw/.  
        • Use relative paths when creating the three new symlinks.  
        • Make sure permissions allow reading the links and CSV files.  
        • The automated tests will 1) check that the directory and file structure matches the
          specification exactly, 2) confirm that the audit log content is byte-for-byte identical,
          3) ensure no broken symlinks are left.

        The entire task can be performed with roughly 5-10 shell commands.
truth:
Initial state (before agent starts):
        Directory /home/user/projects/datasets/raw/ exists and contains three
        regular files with arbitrary CSV content:
        ├── sales_2020.csv
        ├── sales_2021.csv
        └── customers_2020.csv

        Directory /home/user/projects/datasets/links/ exists and contains two symlinks:
        1. /home/user/projects/datasets/links/old_sales
           → ../raw/sales_2019.csv   (broken target, file not present)
        2. /home/user/projects/datasets/links/cust_link
           → ../raw/customers_2020.csv  (valid target)

        The automated checker will expect the following final state:

        1. Directory /home/user/projects/datasets/clean_links/ exists.
        2. It holds exactly these three symlinks (no extras):
           a) /home/user/projects/datasets/clean_links/clean_sales_2020
              – is a symlink whose link text is "../raw/sales_2020.csv"
              – resolves to the *existing* absolute path
                "/home/user/projects/datasets/raw/sales_2020.csv"
           b) /home/user/projects/datasets/clean_links/clean_sales_2021
              – link text "../raw/sales_2021.csv"
              – resolves to "/home/user/projects/datasets/raw/sales_2021.csv"
           c) /home/user/projects/datasets/clean_links/clean_customers_2020
              – link text "../raw/customers_2020.csv"
              – resolves to "/home/user/projects/datasets/raw/customers_2020.csv"

        3. Directory /home/user/projects/datasets/links/ no longer exists
           (deleted recursively).

        4. File /home/user/projects/link_audit.log exists and contains exactly three
           lines, sorted, each line formatted with a single TAB before and after "->".
           Expected byte-for-byte content (final newline at end of file):

/home/user/projects/datasets/clean_links/clean_customers_2020<TAB>-><TAB>/home/user/projects/datasets/raw/customers_2020.csv<NEWLINE>
/home/user/projects/datasets/clean_links/clean_sales_2020<TAB>-><TAB>/home/user/projects/datasets/raw/sales_2020.csv<NEWLINE>
/home/user/projects/datasets/clean_links/clean_sales_2021<TAB>-><TAB>/home/user/projects/datasets/raw/sales_2021.csv<NEWLINE>

        5. There must be zero broken symlinks anywhere under /home/user/projects
           (the checker will run 'find -L /home/user/projects -type l' and expect no output).
